title: The Innovator's Dilemma
author: Clayton Christensen
notes: One of the most important books on understanding why successful companies fail. It shows that many times, in order to survive, you need to embrace your own cannibalization, hence the dilemma of the innovator. Christensen's framework for disruptive innovation changed how I think about competitive strategy at Bemobi

title: Zero to One
author: Peter Thiel
notes: Thiel's contrarian thinking on startups and monopolies. His emphasis on creating something genuinely new rather than incremental improvement resonates deeply with how we built Bemobi.

title: Sapiens
author: Yuval Noah Harari
notes: A sweeping history of humankind that puts technology and business into a much broader perspective. Essential reading for anyone trying to understand where we're headed.

title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
author: Ben Horowitz
notes: The most honest book I've read about the challenges of being a CEO. Horowitz doesn't sugarcoat the difficulties — and that's exactly what makes it valuable.

title: Thinking, Fast and Slow
author: Daniel Kahneman
notes: Understanding cognitive biases has made me a better decision-maker. Kahneman's work on how we think is fundamental for anyone leading organizations.

title: Blitzscaling
author: Reid Hoffman & Chris Yeh
notes: Hoffman captures the trade-offs of scaling fast. Not every lesson applies to every market, but the framework for thinking about growth stages is invaluable.

title: The Lean Startup
author: Eric Ries
notes: The build-measure-learn loop is deceptively simple but incredibly powerful. This methodology shaped how we approach product development at Bemobi.

title: AI Superpowers
author: Kai-Fu Lee
notes: Lee provides an insider's perspective on AI development in China and the US. His analysis of how AI will reshape economies is especially relevant for emerging markets.

title: If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies: Why Superhuman AI Would Kill Us All
author: Eliezer Yudkowsky
notes: As someone deeply invested in AI and its potential, this book challenged me to think seriously about the risks alongside the opportunities. Yudkowsky's arguments are provocative — you don't have to agree with everything, but you can't ignore the questions he raises.

title: Transcend: The New Science of Self-Actualization
author: Scott Barry Kaufman PhD
notes: Kaufman takes Maslow's hierarchy and brings it into the 21st century. I found it genuinely useful for thinking about personal growth — not just as a CEO, but as a father and as someone always trying to evolve.

title: When Everyone Knows That Everyone Knows . . .: Common Knowledge and the Mysteries of Money, Power, and Everyday Life
author: Steven Pinker
notes: Pinker has a way of making you see familiar things with completely fresh eyes. The concept of common knowledge — what happens when everyone knows that everyone knows something — has real implications for how markets work, how trust is built, and how companies communicate.

title: Meditations for Mortals: Four Weeks to Embrace Your Limitations and Make Time for What Counts
author: Oliver Burkeman
notes: Running a public company across 50+ countries doesn't leave much room for reflection. Burkeman reminded me that productivity isn't the point — being intentional about what matters is. A book I keep coming back to.

title: Atmosphere: A GMA Book Club Pick: A Love Story
author: Taylor Jenkins Reid
notes: Sometimes you need a book that has nothing to do with business. Taylor Jenkins Reid writes beautifully, and this one pulled me in completely. A great reminder that storytelling is one of the most powerful human skills.

title: The House of Morgan: An American Banking Dynasty and the Rise of Modern Finance
author: Ron Chernow
notes: Chernow's deep dive into the Morgan banking dynasty is basically a masterclass in how finance shaped the modern world. As someone building a digital payments company, understanding the roots of financial infrastructure gave me a lot of perspective.

title: Extreme Ownership: How U.S. Navy SEALs Lead and Win
author: Jocko Willink, Leif Babin
notes: The core idea — that leaders must own everything in their world — sounds simple, but it's transformative when you actually practice it. I've applied this mindset directly to how I lead at Bemobi, especially during tough moments.

title: All Systems Red
author: Martha Wells
notes: A short, fun sci-fi read about a security robot that just wants to watch soap operas. Beyond the entertainment, there's something interesting here about autonomy, purpose, and what it means to be 'programmed' for one thing but drawn to another. As an AI enthusiast, I couldn't resist this one.

title: The Missing Half: A Novel
author: Ashley Flowers
notes: A compelling novel that explores the gaps in how we understand the people closest to us. Fiction like this keeps me grounded — it's a different kind of learning from business books, but equally important.

title: The Hidden Girl and Other Stories
author: Ken Liu
notes: Ken Liu writes science fiction that's deeply human. These stories explore technology, culture, and identity in ways that made me think about the intersection of innovation and ethics — something very relevant to building tech in emerging markets.

title: The Art of Spending Money: Simple Choices for a Richer Life
author: Morgan Housel
notes: Morgan Housel always delivers clarity on complex topics. This one is about the psychology behind how we spend, save, and think about money. Practical and surprisingly philosophical.

title: The New China Playbook: Beyond Socialism and Capitalism
author: Keyu Jin
notes: Keyu Jin provides an insider's view of China's economic model that goes beyond Western stereotypes. For anyone operating globally — as we do at Bemobi across 50+ countries — understanding China's approach to tech and markets is essential.

title: The Happiness Hypothesis: Finding Modern Truth in Ancient Wisdom
author: Jonathan Haidt
notes: Haidt bridges ancient philosophy and modern psychology in a way that's both rigorous and practical. This book helped me think about happiness not as a destination but as a practice — something I try to carry into both work and family life.

title: Solve for Happy: Engineer Your Path to Joy
author: Mo Gawdat
notes: Written by a former Google executive after a devastating personal loss. Mo Gawdat approaches happiness like an engineering problem, which resonated with my computer engineering background. A deeply moving and practical book.

title: Taste: My Life Through Food
author: Stanley Tucci
notes: Stanley Tucci writing about food and life is pure joy. Nothing to do with tech — just a beautifully written memoir about passion, family, and the pleasures of a well-lived life. As a Brazilian who loves good food, this one hit home.

title: Dotcom Secrets: The Underground Playbook for Growing Your Company Online with Sales Funnels
author: Russell Brunson
notes: Very tactical and specific to online marketing. Not every idea applies to enterprise B2B like Bemobi, but understanding how digital funnels work has influenced how I think about customer journeys and conversion in our platforms.

title: De volta ao jogo: A história de sucesso, dramas e viradas do BTG Pactual
author: Ariane Abdallah
notes: A história do BTG Pactual é uma das mais fascinantes do mercado financeiro brasileiro. Como empreendedor no Brasil, ver os bastidores de como um banco de investimentos navegou crises e se reinventou é ao mesmo tempo inspirador e instrutivo.

title: The Thinking Machine: Jensen Huang, Nvidia, and the World's Most Coveted Microchip
author: Stephen Witt
notes: The story of Jensen Huang and Nvidia is essentially the story of how AI became possible. As someone deeply excited about AI's potential for digital payments and enterprise software, understanding the hardware layer that makes it all work is fascinating.

title: The Art of Living: Peace and Freedom in the Here and Now
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
notes: Thich Nhat Hanh writes with a simplicity that's almost impossible to resist. In a world of constant noise — especially running a global company — this book is a necessary pause. I come back to it when I need to reset.

title: Billion Dollar Whale: The Man Who Fooled Wall Street, Hollywood, and the World
author: Tom Wright, Bradley Hope
notes: An unbelievable true story of fraud on a massive scale. As someone in fintech, it's a stark reminder of why governance, compliance, and transparency matter — and how easily systems can be exploited when those guardrails fail.

title: Determined: A Science of Life Without Free Will
author: Robert M. Sapolsky
notes: Sapolsky makes a compelling case that free will is an illusion — and somehow it's liberating rather than depressing. As someone who makes high-stakes decisions daily, this book made me rethink what 'choice' really means.

title: Abundance
author: Peter H. Diamandis, Steven Kotler
notes: A look at how technology can solve humanity's biggest problems. The optimism here is infectious and aligns with why I got into tech in the first place — the belief that innovation can genuinely improve lives at scale.

title: Careless People: A Cautionary Tale of Power, Greed, and Lost Idealism
author: Sarah Wynn-Williams
notes: A sobering reminder of what happens when power goes unchecked. In the tech world, where things move fast and oversight can lag behind, stories like this are essential reading.

title: I Hate the Ivy League: Riffs and Rants on Elite Education
author: Malcolm Gladwell
notes: Gladwell at his most provocative. Having studied at PUC-Rio and later at Harvard, I found myself nodding and disagreeing in equal measure. That's the mark of a good argument.

title: The Let Them Theory: A Life-Changing Tool That Millions of People Can’t Stop Talking About
author: Mel Robbins
notes: Simple premise, profound impact. Learning to let go of what others do and focusing on what I can control has been transformative — both as a CEO and as a parent.

title: The Year of Living Danishly: Uncovering the Secrets of the World's Happiest Country
author: Helen Russell
notes: A charming exploration of why Denmark consistently ranks as one of the happiest countries. Made me think about work-life balance and what we can learn from cultures that prioritize well-being.

title: What It Takes: Lessons in the Pursuit of Excellence
author: Stephen A. Schwarzman
notes: Schwarzman's journey building Blackstone is a masterclass in ambition and execution. His insights on dealmaking and leadership resonate deeply with building Bemobi from scratch.

title: Money: The True Story of a Made-Up Thing
author: Jacob Goldstein
notes: Goldstein traces the history of money from shells to crypto. For someone building digital payment infrastructure, understanding money's evolution isn't just interesting — it's essential context.

title: The Beginning of Infinity: Explanations That Transform the World
author: David Deutsch
notes: David Deutsch is a brilliant thinker. His argument that human progress comes from good explanations — not resources or luck — is one of the most optimistic and rigorous frameworks I've encountered.

title: Glucose Revolution: The Life-Changing Power of Balancing Your Blood Sugar
author: Jessie Inchauspe
notes: Practical and science-backed. Managing energy is as important as managing time, especially when you're running a company across multiple time zones.

title: Ego Is the Enemy
author: Ryan Holiday
notes: Ryan Holiday distills Stoic wisdom into something actionable. The ego traps he describes — I've seen them in myself and in others. A book I've recommended to my leadership team.

title: Only the Paranoid Survive: How to Exploit the Crisis Points That Challenge Every Company
author: Andrew S. Grove
notes: Andy Grove's classic on strategic inflection points. At Bemobi, we've navigated several of these — from mobile services to digital payments. This book is the playbook.

title: Service Model
author: Adrian Tchaikovsky
notes: Tchaikovsky imagines a world of automated service — funny, dark, and surprisingly thought-provoking. As AI reshapes how enterprises operate, this fiction felt closer to reality than I expected.

title: The Mercy of Gods: Captive's War, Book 1
author: James S. A. Corey
notes: The Expanse authors venture into new territory. Great sci-fi that explores power, survival, and adaptation — themes that resonate beyond fiction.

title: Nexus: A Brief History of Information Networks from the Stone Age to AI
author: Yuval Noah Harari
notes: Harari turns his lens to information networks and how they've shaped civilization. Given that Bemobi is essentially an information and payments network spanning 50+ countries, this felt personally relevant.

title: Young Forever: The Secrets to Living Your Longest, Healthiest Life
author: Mark Hyman MD
notes: Dr. Hyman's functional medicine approach to longevity. With three kids and a demanding career, investing in health isn't optional — it's strategy.

title: Why We Die: The New Science of Aging and the Quest for Immortality
author: Venki Ramakrishnan
notes: Ramakrishnan — a Nobel laureate — explains the biology of aging with remarkable clarity. Fascinating science that makes you think about legacy in a very concrete way.

title: Bad Therapy: Why the Kids Aren't Growing Up
author: Abigail Shrier
notes: A provocative argument about overprotecting children. As a father of three, this challenged some of my assumptions about parenting and resilience.

title: Supercommunicators: How to Unlock the Secret Language of Connection
author: Charles Duhigg
notes: Duhigg reveals the patterns behind great communication. Whether you're negotiating a deal or having dinner with your kids, the principles here are universal.

title: The Anxious Generation: How the Great Rewiring of Childhood Is Causing an Epidemic of Mental Illness
author: Jonathan Haidt
notes: Haidt's research on phones and kids is alarming and important. As a tech executive and a parent, I feel the tension between building digital products and protecting my children from their effects.

title: Behave: The Biology of Humans at Our Best and Worst
author: Robert M. Sapolsky
notes: Sapolsky's magnum opus on human behavior. Dense but rewarding — it changed how I think about why people do what they do, which is useful whether you're managing teams or raising kids.

title: Your Next Five Moves: Master the Art of Business Strategy
author: Patrick Bet-David
notes: Bet-David thinks about strategy like a chess player. Practical and direct — the kind of book you can apply on Monday morning.

title: God, Human, Animal, Machine: Technology, Metaphor, and the Search for Meaning
author: Meghan O'Gieblyn
notes: A philosophical exploration of what it means to be human in the age of AI. As an AI enthusiast, these questions aren't abstract to me — they're becoming central to how we build technology.

title: Unreasonable Hospitality: The Remarkable Power of Giving People More than They Expect
author: Will Guidara
notes: Guidara's approach to service excellence is extraordinary. The principles translate directly to how we should think about customer experience at Bemobi — going beyond what's expected.

title: Dumb Money: The GameStop Short Squeeze and the Ragtag Group of Amateur Traders That Brought Wall Street to Its Knees (Previously Published as The Antisocial Network)
author: Ben Mezrich
notes: A wild ride through the GameStop saga. As someone in fintech, watching retail investors challenge Wall Street was both thrilling and a preview of how finance is being democratized.

title: The Creative Act: A Way of Being
author: Rick Rubin
notes: Rick Rubin's philosophy of creativity applies far beyond music. His ideas about attention, process, and trust in your own instincts are relevant to anyone building something new.

title: Immune: A Journey into the Mysterious System That Keeps You Alive
author: Philipp Dettmer
notes: Kurzgesagt's creator explains immunology in the most engaging way possible. Complex science made accessible — something I aspire to do when explaining fintech to non-technical audiences.

title: If You're in My Office, It's Already Too Late: A Divorce Lawyer's Guide to Staying Together
author: James J. Sexton
notes: An unconventional relationship book from a divorce lawyer. Surprisingly useful insights about communication, expectations, and the small things that compound over time.

title: Same as Ever: A Guide to What Never Changes
author: Morgan Housel
notes: Morgan Housel's best work. While everyone obsesses over what's changing, this book focuses on what stays the same — human behavior, risk, and the patterns that repeat across centuries.

title: Going Infinite: The Rise and Fall of a New Tycoon
author: Michael Lewis
notes: Michael Lewis chronicles Sam Bankman-Fried's spectacular rise and fall. A cautionary tale about what happens when growth outpaces governance — something every fintech leader should read.

title: Build: An Unorthodox Guide to Making Things Worth Making
author: Tony Fadell
notes: Tony Fadell — the guy who built the iPod and Nest — shares practical wisdom about building products and teams. Raw, honest, and directly applicable.

title: The End of the World Is Just the Beginning: Mapping the Collapse of Globalization
author: Peter Zeihan
notes: Zeihan's geopolitical analysis of deglobalization is provocative. His predictions about supply chains and demographics directly impact how I think about Bemobi's international strategy.

title: Four Thousand Weeks: Time Management for Mortals
author: Oliver Burkeman
notes: Burkeman argues that conventional productivity advice is a trap. With only 4,000 weeks in a human life, the question isn't how to do more — it's how to choose what matters. A book I think about often.

title: Amp It Up: Leading for Hypergrowth by Raising Expectations, Increasing Urgency, and Elevating Intensity
author: Frank Slootman
notes: Slootman's playbook for high-growth leadership. His intensity isn't for everyone, but the core message — raise the bar, increase urgency — is something I practice at Bemobi.

title: Destined for War: Can America and China Escape Thucydides's Trap?
author: Graham Allison
notes: Allison's analysis of great power competition is essential geopolitical reading. Operating across 50+ countries means understanding these dynamics isn't optional.

title: Elon Musk
author: Walter Isaacson
notes: Love him or not, Musk's story is extraordinary. The relentless drive and willingness to bet everything on a vision — that's something any entrepreneur can relate to, even if at a different scale.

title: Born Standing Up: A Comic's Life
author: Steve Martin
notes: Steve Martin's memoir about his years in stand-up is actually a masterclass in craft and perseverance. The years of deliberate practice before 'overnight success' — that's entrepreneurship too.

title: Freezing Order: A True Story of Russian Money Laundering, State-Sponsored Murder, and Surviving Vladimir Putin's Wrath
author: Bill Browder
notes: Bill Browder's sequel to Red Notice. A gripping account of fighting corruption at the highest levels. Reinforces why transparency in financial systems — like what we build at Bemobi — matters so much.

title: The Coming Wave: AI, Power, and Our Future
author: Mustafa Suleyman, Michael Bhaskar
notes: Mustafa Suleyman's warning about AI and biotech is both exciting and sobering. As someone building AI-powered enterprise solutions, I take his call for containment seriously.

title: Burn Rate: Launching a Startup and Losing My Mind
author: Andy Dunn
notes: Andy Dunn's honest account of building Bonobos while struggling with bipolar disorder. A rare look at the mental health cost of entrepreneurship. Brave and important.

title: Understanding Japan: A Cultural History
author: Mark J. Ravina
notes: Japan's ability to blend deep tradition with cutting-edge innovation fascinates me. Understanding this culture has helped me think about how technology adoption varies across the 50+ countries where Bemobi operates.

title: The Epigenetics Revolution: How Modern Biology Is Rewriting Our Understanding of Genetics, Disease, and Inheritance
author: Nessa Carey
notes: The idea that our genes aren't our destiny — that environment and behavior can change gene expression — is one of the most fascinating scientific developments of our time.

title: Think Again: The Power of Knowing What You Don't Know
author: Adam Grant
notes: Adam Grant makes the case for intellectual humility. In a fast-moving industry like fintech, the ability to unlearn and rethink is probably the most valuable skill.

title: Children of Ruin
author: Adrian Tchaikovsky
notes: Tchaikovsky's sequel expands the scope of the first book beautifully. Thoughtful sci-fi about what intelligence might look like in forms completely different from our own.

title: Columbus Day: Expeditionary Force, Book 1
author: Craig Alanson
notes: Pure fun. Military sci-fi with a great sense of humor. Sometimes after a week of board meetings and earnings calls, you just need a good space adventure.

title: What's Our Problem?: A Self-Help Book for Societies
author: Tim Urban
notes: Tim Urban's ambitious attempt to explain why society seems so broken. His framework for thinking about thinking is genuinely useful — I found myself applying it to team dynamics at work.

title: Tomorrow, and Tomorrow, and Tomorrow: A Novel
author: Gabrielle Zevin
notes: A beautiful novel about creativity, collaboration, and the games we play — literally and figuratively. One of the best fiction reads I've had in years.

title: Verity
author: Colleen Hoover
notes: A psychological thriller that kept me up way past my bedtime. Pure entertainment, no business angle — and that's exactly why I loved it.

title: The Algebra of Happiness: Notes on the Pursuit of Success, Love, and Meaning
author: Scott Galloway
notes: Scott Galloway's blunt, data-driven take on what actually makes people happy. Short, punchy, and surprisingly moving.

title: Outlive: The Science and Art of Longevity
author: Peter Attia, Bill Gifford
notes: Peter Attia's comprehensive guide to living longer and better. The section on exercise as medicine changed my routine. At 50+, this isn't theoretical anymore.

title: Influence, New and Expanded: The Psychology of Persuasion
author: Robert B. Cialdini
notes: Cialdini's classic, updated. Understanding the psychology of influence is critical in business — from sales to negotiations to building partnerships across cultures.

title: Breath: The New Science of a Lost Art
author: James Nestor
notes: Nestor's exploration of how something as basic as breathing can transform health. I started the breathing exercises during the book and haven't stopped.

title: Chip War: The Quest to Dominate the World's Most Critical Technology
author: Chris Miller
notes: Chris Miller tells the story of semiconductors as geopolitics. Understanding the chip supply chain is crucial for anyone in tech — it's the foundation everything else is built on.

title: Surrender: 40 Songs, One Story
author: Bono
notes: Bono's memoir is surprisingly reflective and humble. The parallels between building a band and building a company — the creative tension, the partnerships, the ego management — resonated more than I expected.

title: The Vital Question: Energy, Evolution, and the Origins of Complex Life
author: Nick Lane
notes: Nick Lane asks fundamental questions about why life exists at all. Dense science, but the kind that expands your worldview permanently.

title: Dark Matter: A Novel
author: Blake Crouch
notes: Blake Crouch writes thrillers that feel scientifically plausible. This one about parallel universes had me thinking about the roads not taken — a fun exercise for any entrepreneur.

title: Catch and Kill: Lies, Spies, and a Conspiracy to Protect Predators
author: Ronan Farrow
notes: Ronan Farrow's investigation is both journalism and thriller. A powerful reminder of why accountability and transparency matter in every institution.

title: The Martian
author: Andy Weir
notes: Andy Weir made engineering exciting. The problem-solving mindset — break it down, work the problem — is exactly how I approach challenges at Bemobi. Also, it's just incredibly fun.

title: A Gift of Time
author: Jerry Merritt
notes: A touching story about what you'd do with a second chance. Made me reflect on priorities — family, impact, the things that truly matter beyond quarterly results.

title: The Redemption of Time: A Three-Body Problem Novel
author: Baoshu
notes: A fan-written sequel that became official. Interesting as both fiction and as an example of how communities can extend great creative work.

title: Waking Up: A Guide to Spirituality Without Religion
author: Sam Harris
notes: Sam Harris bridges neuroscience and meditation in a way that appeals to the analytically minded. I started a regular meditation practice after reading this.

title: Making Sense: Conversations on Consciousness, Morality, and the Future of Humanity
author: Sam Harris
notes: Harris's conversations with thinkers across disciplines. Each chapter challenges a different assumption. The kind of book that makes you smarter just by engaging with it.

title: Cloud Atlas (20th Anniversary Edition): A Novel
author: David Mitchell
notes: David Mitchell's masterpiece — six interconnected stories spanning centuries. A stunning demonstration of how everything is connected, which is something I believe about business and technology too.

title: The Power Law: Venture Capital and the Making of the New Future
author: Sebastian Mallaby
notes: Mallaby's definitive history of venture capital. As someone who's both raised VC money and invested in startups, this book captured the world I've lived in with remarkable accuracy.

title: The War on the West
author: Douglas Murray
notes: Douglas Murray's defense of Western civilization is controversial and thought-provoking. I don't agree with everything, but the arguments are worth engaging with seriously.

title: Woke, Inc.: Inside Corporate America's Social Justice Scam
author: Vivek Ramaswamy
notes: Ramaswamy challenges corporate activism. Whether you agree or not, the questions he raises about the role of business in society are ones every CEO should grapple with.

title: Predictably Irrational: The Hidden Forces That Shape Our Decisions
author: Dan Ariely
notes: Ariely shows how systematically irrational we all are. Essential reading for anyone designing products or services — understanding biases is understanding your customers.

title: Why We're Polarized
author: Ezra Klein
notes: Ezra Klein explains the structural forces driving political division. Understanding polarization helps me navigate the complex regulatory and political environments across the countries where Bemobi operates.

title: Play Nice But Win: A CEO's Journey from Founder to Leader
author: Michael Dell, James Kaplan
notes: Michael Dell's story of building, losing, and reclaiming his company. The comeback arc is inspiring, and his lessons about evolving as a leader mirror challenges I've faced.

title: Children of Time: Children of Time, Book 1
author: Adrian Tchaikovsky
notes: Tchaikovsky's best — a story about evolution, intelligence, and what it means to build a civilization. The sci-fi that stays with you and changes how you think.

title: The God Equation: The Quest for a Theory of Everything
author: Michio Kaku
notes: Kaku makes theoretical physics accessible and exciting. The quest for a unified theory mirrors the entrepreneurial drive to find elegant solutions to complex problems.

title: The Big Nine: How the Tech Titans and Their Thinking Machines Could Warp Humanity
author: Amy Webb
notes: Amy Webb maps out how nine companies are shaping the future of AI. As someone betting on AI in enterprise, understanding the competitive landscape at the macro level is vital.

title: Super Pumped: The Battle for Uber
author: Mike Isaac
notes: The Uber story is a case study in hypergrowth gone wrong. The culture problems, the governance failures — every startup founder should read this as a cautionary tale.

title: Ageless: The New Science of Getting Older Without Getting Old
author: Andrew Steele
notes: Andrew Steele makes the science of aging feel hopeful rather than fatalistic. A book that shifts your perspective from aging as inevitable decline to aging as a problem to solve.

title: The Three Laws of Performance: Rewriting the Future of Your Organization and Your Life
author: Steve Zaffron, Dave Logan
notes: A practical framework for organizational transformation. The insight that performance is driven by how situations 'occur' to people has changed how I approach culture at Bemobi.

title: Kings of Crypto
author: Jeff John Roberts
notes: The story of Coinbase and the crypto industry's early days. Relevant context for anyone in fintech watching how digital assets are reshaping payments and finance.

title: Bad Blood: Secrets and Lies in a Silicon Valley Startup
author: John Carreyrou
notes: Theranos is the ultimate cautionary tale. As a public company CEO, this book reinforced my belief that transparency and integrity aren't negotiable — they're the foundation.

title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
author: Douglas Adams
notes: Douglas Adams' genius is timeless. 'Don't Panic' is honestly not bad advice for running a company either.

title: Stories of Your Life and Others
author: Ted Chiang
notes: Ted Chiang writes science fiction that's really philosophy in disguise. 'Story of Your Life' — the basis for the film Arrival — is one of the most beautiful things I've ever read.

title: Amazon Unbound: Jeff Bezos and the Invention of a Global Empire
author: Brad Stone
notes: Brad Stone's deep dive into Amazon's expansion. The scale of ambition and execution is humbling. Many lessons here about building platforms — something we do at Bemobi in a different domain.

title: Exhalation: Stories
author: Ted Chiang
notes: More Ted Chiang brilliance. Each story is a thought experiment about consciousness, time, or free will, wrapped in beautiful prose. The kind of fiction that makes you a better thinker.

title: American Kingpin: The Epic Hunt for the Criminal Mastermind Behind the Silk Road
author: Nick Bilton
notes: The Silk Road story reads like a thriller. As someone in digital payments, it's a reminder that technology is neutral — what matters is what we build with it.

title: Once Upon a Time in Russia: The Rise of the Oligarchs and the Greatest Wealth in History
author: Ben Mezrich
notes: Ben Mezrich chronicles the wild west of Russian capitalism. Understanding how markets and power structures form in emerging economies gives me perspective on the markets where Bemobi operates.

title: Red Notice: A True Story of High Finance, Murder and One Man's Fight for Justice
author: Bill Browder
notes: Bill Browder's incredible story of investing in Russia and fighting corruption. Gripping, terrifying, and a testament to the importance of rule of law for business.

title: How to Change Your Mind: What the New Science of Psychedelics Teaches Us About Consciousness, Dying, Addiction, Depression, and Transcendence
author: Michael Pollan
notes: Pollan's exploration of psychedelics and consciousness is fascinating. The research on neuroplasticity and mental health is genuinely groundbreaking.

title: Principles for Dealing with the Changing World Order: Why Nations Succeed or Fail
author: Ray Dalio
notes: Dalio's macro framework for understanding how empires rise and fall. Essential context for anyone making long-term bets across global markets.

title: Has China Won?: The Chinese Challenge to American Primacy
author: Kishore Mahbubani
notes: Mahbubani offers an Asian perspective on the US-China competition. Operating in 50+ countries means I need to understand both sides of this story.

title: A Man Called Ove: A Novel
author: Fredrik Backman
notes: A grumpy old man and the neighbors who change his life. Funny, tender, and a reminder that the best things in life are the connections we make with other people.

title: A Thousand Brains: A New Theory of Intelligence
author: Jeff Hawkins
notes: Jeff Hawkins proposes a new framework for understanding the brain and building AI. As an AI enthusiast, his theory of reference frames is one of the most interesting ideas I've encountered.

title: The Outsiders: Eight Unconventional CEOs and Their Radically Rational Blueprint for Success
author: William N. Thorndike
notes: Thorndike profiles CEOs who delivered extraordinary returns by thinking differently about capital allocation. A book I've revisited multiple times — it directly influences how I think about capital deployment at Bemobi.

title: Endurance: Shackleton's Incredible Voyage
author: Alfred Lansing
notes: The ultimate leadership story. Shackleton's ability to keep his crew alive and motivated in impossible conditions sets the standard for what leadership under pressure looks like.

title: Lights Out: A Cyberattack, A Nation Unprepared, Surviving the Aftermath
author: Ted Koppel
notes: Koppel explores what would happen if a cyberattack took down the US power grid. As someone building digital infrastructure, cybersecurity isn't abstract — it's existential.

title: The Molecule of More: How a Single Chemical in Your Brain Drives Love, Sex, and Creativity - And Will Determine the Fate of the Human Race
author: Daniel Z. Lieberman, Michael E. Long
notes: A fascinating look at how dopamine shapes ambition, creativity, and addiction. Explains a lot about entrepreneurial drive — and its dark side.

title: What You Do Is Who You Are: How to Create Your Business Culture
author: Ben Horowitz
notes: Ben Horowitz's guide to building culture. I've used frameworks from this book directly in shaping Bemobi's culture across our global offices.

title: Cien años de soledad
author: Gabriel García Márquez
notes: García Márquez's masterpiece. Reading it in Spanish adds a layer of magic that's hard to describe. A reminder of the richness of Latin American storytelling and identity.

title: AI 2041: Ten Visions for Our Future
author: Kai-Fu Lee, Chen Qiufan
notes: Kai-Fu Lee and Chen Qiufan blend fiction with analysis to imagine AI's near future. Each story is paired with a technical essay — a creative format that makes complex ideas accessible.

title: Mission Economy: A Moonshot Guide to Changing Capitalism
author: Mariana Mazzucato
notes: Mazzucato argues for a more active role of government in driving innovation. An important counterpoint to the pure market view, and relevant to how we think about public-private partnerships in fintech.

title: Being Mortal: Medicine and What Matters in the End
author: Atul Gawande
notes: Atul Gawande writes about death and aging with extraordinary compassion. Changed how I think about what it means to live well — not just live long.

title: When Breath Becomes Air
author: Paul Kalanithi
notes: Paul Kalanithi's memoir, written as he was dying of cancer, is devastating and beautiful. A book that puts everything — work, ambition, success — into perspective.

title: All These Worlds: Bobiverse, Book 3
author: Dennis E. Taylor
notes: The Bobiverse trilogy wraps up brilliantly. Hard sci-fi with heart and humor — the rare series that gets better with each installment.

title: For We Are Many: Bobiverse, Book 2
author: Dennis E. Taylor
notes: The Bobiverse expands in scope and complexity. Dennis Taylor manages to make self-replicating space probes feel deeply human.

title: We Are Legion (We Are Bob): Bobiverse, Book 1
author: Dennis E. Taylor
notes: A software engineer gets uploaded into a space probe. The premise sounds absurd but the execution is brilliant. One of the most entertaining sci-fi series I've read.

title: Numbers Don't Lie: 71 Stories to Help Us Understand the Modern World
author: Vaclav Smil
notes: Vaclav Smil uses data to cut through noise. His ability to quantify the world we live in is unmatched. Bill Gates recommends him for a reason.

title: Origin Story: A Big History of Everything
author: David Christian
notes: David Christian tells the story of everything — from the Big Bang to the present. The ultimate zoom-out that puts daily business challenges in humbling perspective.

title: One Hundred Years of Solitude
author: Gabriel Garcia Marquez
notes: Reading this in English after having read it in Spanish — both versions are extraordinary. García Márquez created a world that feels more real than reality.

title: The Omnivore's Dilemma: A Natural History of Four Meals
author: Michael Pollan
notes: Pollan traces four meals from origin to plate. Changed how I think about food systems — and systems thinking in general.

title: Artemis
author: Andy Weir
notes: Andy Weir's lunar city thriller. Lighter than The Martian but still packed with that engineering-problem-solving spirit I love.

title: The Future of Humanity: Terraforming Mars, Interstellar Travel, Immortality, and Our Destiny Beyond Earth
author: Michio Kaku
notes: Kaku's vision of humanity's future in space. Optimistic, ambitious, and the kind of long-term thinking that makes you excited about what technology can achieve.

title: The Midnight Library: A GMA Book Club Pick (A Novel)
author: Matt Haig
notes: A novel about all the lives you could have lived. Moving and thought-provoking — made me grateful for the choices I've made, even the difficult ones.

title: Project Hail Mary
author: Andy Weir
notes: Andy Weir's best book. The science, the humor, the friendship at its core — this one genuinely moved me. If you read one sci-fi novel, make it this one.

title: Range: Why Generalists Triumph in a Specialized World
author: David Epstein
notes: Epstein makes the case for being a generalist. As someone who went from computer engineering to Cisco to Oi to building Bemobi, this book validated a career path that wasn't always conventional.

title: How Will You Measure Your Life?
author: Clayton M. Christensen, James Allworth, Karen Dillon
notes: Clayton Christensen applies business frameworks to life's biggest questions. His insight that the metrics of a meaningful life are different from the metrics of a successful career has stayed with me.

title: Noise: A Flaw in Human Judgment
author: Daniel Kahneman, Olivier Sibony, Cass R. Sunstein
notes: Kahneman, Sibony, and Sunstein expose how inconsistent human judgment really is. For anyone making decisions that affect others — hiring, investing, strategy — this is essential reading.

title: Everybody Lies: Big Data, New Data, and What the Internet Can Tell Us About Who We Really Are
author: Seth Stephens-Davidowitz
notes: Stephens-Davidowitz uses search data to reveal what people actually think. Fascinating and occasionally unsettling — a reminder of the power and responsibility that comes with data.

title: Kafka on the Shore
author: Haruki Murakami
notes: Murakami's surreal masterpiece. Reading it is like dreaming while awake. The kind of book that resists explanation but leaves a permanent mark.

title: Principles: Life and Work
author: Ray Dalio
notes: Dalio's systematic approach to decision-making. I don't agree with every principle, but the discipline of codifying your beliefs and testing them against reality is incredibly valuable.

title: On the Future: Prospects for Humanity
author: Martin Rees
notes: Martin Rees — the UK's Astronomer Royal — on the existential risks and opportunities facing humanity. Sober, scientific, and urgent.

title: Foundation's Edge
author: Isaac Asimov
notes: Asimov expanding his Foundation universe. The questions about free will and determinism in these books feel increasingly relevant in the age of AI.

title: The Code Breaker: Jennifer Doudna, Gene Editing, and the Future of the Human Race
author: Walter Isaacson
notes: Isaacson tells the CRISPR story through Jennifer Doudna's life. The intersection of science, ethics, and entrepreneurship — themes that run through my own work in tech.

title: Second Foundation
author: Isaac Asimov
notes: The second twist in Asimov's trilogy. His vision of psychohistory — predicting the behavior of large populations — feels like a predecessor to big data and AI.

title: Foundation and Empire
author: Isaac Asimov
notes: Asimov at his best — the tension between individual agency and historical forces. These books shaped my love of science fiction as a teenager and still hold up decades later.

title: Foundation
author: Isaac Asimov
notes: Where it all started. Asimov's Foundation series is the reason I fell in love with science fiction. The idea that knowledge and planning can save civilization — that's the ultimate optimistic vision.

title: Skin in the Game: Hidden Asymmetries in Daily Life
author: Nassim Nicholas Taleb
notes: Taleb's argument that you should never trust anyone who doesn't have skin in the game. As a founder and CEO with significant equity in Bemobi, this principle is something I live every day.

title: Surely You're Joking, Mr. Feynman!
author: Richard P. Feynman
notes: Feynman's curiosity and irreverence are infectious. His approach to learning — always questioning, always experimenting — is something I try to model.

title: How to Avoid a Climate Disaster: The Solutions We Have and the Breakthroughs We Need
author: Bill Gates
notes: Gates provides a clear-eyed roadmap for climate action. The engineer's approach to a massive problem — break it down, find leverage points — is exactly right.

title: The Power of Vulnerability: Teachings on Authenticity, Connection, and Courage
author: Brené Brown PhD LMSW
notes: Brené Brown's research on vulnerability changed how I think about leadership. Showing up authentically isn't weakness — it's what builds real trust in teams.

title: The Ministry for the Future: A Novel
author: Kim Stanley Robinson
notes: Kim Stanley Robinson imagines how humanity might actually solve climate change. Ambitious, detailed, and surprisingly hopeful. The most important sci-fi novel of the decade.

title: Seveneves: A Novel
author: Neal Stephenson
notes: Stephenson imagines humanity's response to the moon exploding. Epic in scope, meticulous in science. The engineering challenges in this book are as engaging as any real-world project.

title: Born a Crime: Stories from a South African Childhood
author: Trevor Noah
notes: Trevor Noah's childhood in apartheid South Africa is funny, harrowing, and inspiring. His ability to find humor in darkness is a genuine superpower.

title: The Buy Side: A Wall Street Trader's Tale of Spectacular Excess
author: Turney Duff
notes: A raw, honest account of Wall Street excess and addiction. A counterpoint to the glamorized version of finance.

title: Stumbling on Happiness
author: Daniel Gilbert
notes: Daniel Gilbert shows how bad we are at predicting what will make us happy. Useful science for anyone trying to make better life decisions.

title: Enlightenment Now: The Case for Reason, Science, Humanism, and Progress
author: Steven Pinker
notes: Pinker makes the data-driven case that the world is getting better. In a time of pessimism, this book is a necessary corrective — and a reminder of why progress matters.

title: Lifespan: Why We Age—and Why We Don't Have To
author: David A. Sinclair, Matthew D. LaPlante
notes: David Sinclair's argument that aging is a disease we can treat. Cutting-edge science that could reshape healthcare — and society — in our lifetime.

title: Lee Kuan Yew: The Grand Master’s Insights on China, United States, and the World
author: Graham Allison, Robert D. Blackwill, Ali Wyne
notes: Lee Kuan Yew built modern Singapore through sheer vision and discipline. His geopolitical insights remain remarkably relevant for anyone operating across Asia.

title: To Hold Up the Sky
author: Cixin Liu
notes: Cixin Liu's short story collection explores humanity's relationship with the cosmos. Each story is a different lens on technology and its consequences.

title: Recursion: A Novel
author: Blake Crouch
notes: Blake Crouch explores memory and identity in a thriller that's impossible to put down. The science feels plausible enough to be unsettling.

title: The Dark Forest
author: Liu Cixin
notes: The second Three-Body Problem book introduces the Dark Forest theory — a chilling answer to the Fermi Paradox. The strategic thinking here applies far beyond astrophysics.

title: Death's End
author: Liu Cixin
notes: The conclusion to the Three-Body Problem trilogy. Cixin Liu's vision is vast and humbling. One of the most ambitious works of fiction I've ever read.

title: No Filter: The Inside Story of Instagram
author: Sarah Frier
notes: Sarah Frier captures how Instagram grew and changed culture. A relevant study in platform building, creator economies, and the tension between growth and values.

title: Ready Player Two: A Novel
author: Ernest Cline
notes: A fun sequel that explores virtual reality and nostalgia. Not as tight as the first, but still an entertaining ride through geek culture.

title: Zero to One: Notes on Startups, or How to Build the Future
author: Peter Thiel, Blake Masters
notes: I've already noted this in my curated list — Peter Thiel's thinking on building something genuinely new rather than copying what exists. Worth reading twice.

title: A Promised Land
author: Barack Obama
notes: Obama's memoir is a thoughtful, detailed look at governance and leadership. The weight of decisions that affect millions — it's a perspective that puts corporate leadership in context.

title: The Three-Body Problem
author: Liu Cixin
notes: Cixin Liu's masterpiece. The scope of imagination here is staggering. This trilogy changed how I think about technology, civilization, and our place in the universe.

title: The Undoing Project: A Friendship that Changed Our Minds
author: Michael Lewis
notes: Michael Lewis tells the story of Kahneman and Tversky's partnership. A beautiful exploration of how two minds working together can change the world.

title: That Will Never Work: The Birth of Netflix and the Amazing Life of an Idea
author: Marc Randolph
notes: Marc Randolph's honest account of Netflix's early days. The chaos, the near-death moments, the pivots — every startup founder will see themselves in this story.

title: The Game: Penetrating the Secret Society of Pickup Artists
author: Neil Strauss
notes: A guilty pleasure from years ago. Strauss's journey is more sociology experiment than dating guide. Interesting as a study of subcultures and persuasion.

title: The Lessons of History
author: Will Durant, Ariel Durant
notes: Durant distills all of human history into 100 pages. Dense with wisdom. The kind of book you reread every few years and find something new each time.

title: The Body: A Guide for Occupants
author: Bill Bryson
notes: Bryson makes the human body endlessly fascinating. His gift for making complex science entertaining and accessible is unmatched.

title: Why We Sleep: Unlocking the Power of Sleep and Dreams
author: Matthew Walker PhD PhD
notes: Walker's research on sleep is eye-opening — literally. After reading this, I made sleep a non-negotiable priority. The performance benefits alone justify it.

title: No Rules Rules: Netflix and the Culture of Reinvention
author: Reed Hastings, Erin Meyer
notes: Hastings and Meyer reveal Netflix's radical culture of freedom and responsibility. Not every practice translates, but the thinking behind it is invaluable for any leader.

title: Good to Great: Why Some Companies Make the Leap...And Others Don't
author: Jim Collins
notes: Collins' research on what separates good companies from great ones. The concepts of Level 5 leadership and the Hedgehog Concept have directly influenced how I lead Bemobi.

title: A Short History of Nearly Everything
author: Bill Bryson
notes: Bryson turns the entire history of science into a page-turner. A reminder of how much we've figured out — and how much we still don't know.

title: The Most Important Thing: Uncommon Sense for The Thoughtful Investor
author: Howard Marks
notes: Howard Marks' investment philosophy is as much about thinking clearly as it is about markets. Second-level thinking — his core concept — applies to every strategic decision.

title: Mindset: The New Psychology of Success
author: Carol S. Dweck
notes: Carol Dweck's growth mindset framework is simple but powerful. I've seen firsthand how fixed vs. growth mindset affects performance in teams.

title: The Gene: An Intimate History
author: Siddhartha Mukherjee
notes: Mukherjee tells the story of genetics with the narrative skill of a novelist. Essential for understanding where biotechnology — and humanity — is headed.

title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things: Building a Business When There Are No Easy Answers
author: Ben Horowitz
notes: Already in my curated list — Horowitz's honesty about the CEO struggle is unmatched. I've bought copies for every executive on my team.

title: The Everything Store: Jeff Bezos and the Age of Amazon
author: Brad Stone
notes: Brad Stone's first Amazon book. Understanding Bezos' long-term thinking and customer obsession has influenced how I think about building Bemobi for the long haul.

title: Shoe Dog: A Memoir by the Creator of Nike
author: Phil Knight
notes: Phil Knight's memoir is raw and real. The early days of Nike — the struggle, the doubt, the near-bankruptcy — mirror every entrepreneur's journey. One of my favorite business books.

title: Radical Focus: Achieving Your Most Important Goals with Objectives and Key Results
author: Christina R. Wodtke
notes: Wodtke makes OKRs practical and human. We've used this framework at Bemobi, and this book captures both the method and the spirit behind it.

title: Measure What Matters: How Google, Bono, and the Gates Foundation Rock the World with OKRs
author: John Doerr
notes: John Doerr's OKR bible. The case studies alone are worth the read — seeing how different organizations apply the same framework is instructive.

title: Elon Musk: Tesla, SpaceX, and the Quest for a Fantastic Future
author: Ashlee Vance
notes: Love him or not, Musk's story is extraordinary. The relentless drive and willingness to bet everything on a vision — that's something any entrepreneur can relate to, even if at a different scale.

title: The Headspace Guide to Meditation and Mindfulness: How Mindfulness Can Change Your Life in Ten Minutes a Day
author: Andy Puddicombe
notes: Andy Puddicombe makes meditation approachable for skeptics. I started using Headspace after this book and it became part of my daily routine.

title: The Man Who Solved the Market: How Jim Simons Launched the Quant Revolution
author: Gregory Zuckerman
notes: Jim Simons and Renaissance Technologies represent the ultimate fusion of mathematics and markets. As someone with an engineering background in finance, this story is endlessly fascinating.

title: Atomic Habits: An Easy & Proven Way to Build Good Habits & Break Bad Ones
author: James Clear
notes: James Clear distills habit science into something immediately actionable. The 1% improvement philosophy is how I think about personal and organizational growth.

title: Outliers: The Story of Success
author: Malcolm Gladwell
notes: Gladwell's exploration of what makes people successful. The 10,000-hour rule gets all the attention, but the deeper insight — that context and opportunity matter as much as talent — is the real lesson.

title: Age of Ambition: Chasing Fortune, Truth, and Faith in the New China
author: Evan Osnos
notes: Evan Osnos captures China's transformation through individual stories. Essential reading for understanding the country that's reshaping global technology and trade.

title: Talking to Strangers: What We Should Know about the People We Don't Know
author: Malcolm Gladwell
notes: Gladwell explores why we're so bad at reading people. The implications for hiring, negotiating, and building trust across cultures are profound.

title: Never Split the Difference: Negotiating as if Your Life Depended on It
author: Chris Voss, Tahl Raz
notes: Chris Voss brings FBI hostage negotiation techniques to everyday life. The tactical empathy framework is something I use in business negotiations constantly.

title: The Ride of a Lifetime: Lessons Learned from 15 Years as CEO of the Walt Disney Company
author: Robert Iger
notes: Bob Iger's memoir of leading Disney through its greatest era of transformation. His approach to acquisitions and creative leadership is a masterclass.

title: 21 Lessons for the 21st Century
author: Yuval Noah Harari
notes: Harari shifts from past and future to the present. Each chapter tackles a different challenge we face right now — technology, politics, meaning. Essential and urgent.

title: Prepared: What Kids Need for a Fulfilled Life
author: Diane Tavenner
notes: Diane Tavenner reimagines education around what kids actually need. As a father of three, this book influenced how I think about preparing my children for the future.

title: Homo Deus: A Brief History of Tomorrow
author: Yuval Noah Harari
notes: Harari looks forward this time — what happens when technology surpasses human capability? The questions he raises about dataism and AI are only becoming more relevant.

title: Deep Medicine: How Artificial Intelligence Can Make Healthcare Human Again
author: Eric Topol
notes: Eric Topol argues that AI can make medicine more human, not less. A hopeful vision that aligns with my belief that technology should serve people, not replace them.
